claim.hypotheses: Lower executive function capacity (larger Stroop interference) and lower Need for Cognition will be associated with a larger reward-induced reduction in task-switch costs (i.e., greater increase in cognitive effort expenditure), whereas higher capacity / higher NFC individuals will be less responsive to reward incentives.
claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction, paragraph beginning “Considering these two separate lines of research together…” through paragraph ending “…we expect that these individuals should exhibit a smaller reward-induced reduction in task switch costs.” (pp. 101-102)
claim.statement: We found that individuals with lower executive function capacity—and relatedly, lower intrinsic motivation to expend effort (Need for Cognition)—showed a larger increase in cognitive effort expenditure in response to monetary reward incentives, while individuals with greater capacity and greater intrinsic motivation were less responsive to reward incentives.

data.source: Laboratory experiment using computerized Stroop task and task-switching paradigm
data.access_details: Raw data available on Open Science Framework: http://osf.io/gqvsh
data.notes: Seven participants excluded for <80% accuracy, two for excessive missed deadlines; two additional participants had missing NFC data for analyses involving that measure.

method.description: Participants completed a Stroop task to measure executive function capacity, followed by two blocks of a card-based task-switching paradigm in which the monetary reward for correct responses was manipulated (High vs Low). Mixed-effects regressions were used to assess how reward, trial type, and individual differences in Stroop interference and Need for Cognition predicted switch costs in reaction time and accuracy.
steps: Recruit 54 adult participants from McGill University and obtain informed consent.
Administer questionnaires: Need for Cognition (18 items), BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7.
Conduct a practice and then a 120-trial computerized Stroop task (90 congruent, 30 incongruent).
Compute individual Stroop RT cost (incongruent − congruent, standardized).
Inform participants about performance-contingent bonuses; run two 140-trial task-switching blocks with reward level counter-balanced (5¢ High, 1¢ Low).
Include filler task and 8-minute music break between blocks.
Exclude participants not meeting performance criteria (<80% accuracy or ≥15 missed deadlines).
Log-transform RTs; analyse correct trials only.

Fit mixed-effects linear (RT) and logistic (accuracy) regressions with lme4 in R, including fixed effects for trial type, reward, block, and interactions with Stroop cost or NFC, plus random effects for subjects.
method.models: Mixed-effects linear regression for log-transformed RT; mixed-effects logistic regression for accuracy.
method.outcome_variable: Task switch cost (difference in RT between switch and repeat trials); reaction time on each trial; response accuracy.
method.independent_variables: Reward condition (High vs Low), individual Stroop RT cost, Need for Cognition score.
method.control_variables: Trial type (switch vs repeat), experimental block (order/practice effects), Behavioral Activation Scale (in some analyses).

results.summary: Reward incentives reduced task-switch costs primarily in individuals with lower executive function capacity and lower Need for Cognition. A significant three-way interaction showed that as Stroop RT cost increased, the reward-induced decrease in switch costs became larger (β = −0.0373, p = .008). Conversely, higher NFC scores were associated with a smaller—or even reversed—reward effect on switch costs (β = 0.0382, p = .008). Reward alone did not significantly affect switch costs at the group level.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Three-way interaction (Trial type × Reward × Stroop RT cost) on log RT
Three-way interaction (Trial type × Reward × NFC) on log RT
Main effect of Reward on switch cost (group level)

metadata.original_paper_title: Cognitive capacity limitations and Need for Cognition differentially predict reward-induced cognitive effort expenditure
